userPrompt: Cluster B.1 — Phase 4b Initialization. Design how raw Phase 4a material (problem statement, Concerns, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Resolve Conditions) types into the proof MCP at the boundary. Six pairings inherited from cluster-B NC-1. Two-channel raw-material model from NC-2 (problem statement explicit; everything else via agent session memory). Concern iteration mechanism from NC-3 with five integration constraints (sequential ratification preserved, late-lock procrastination gate, merge evidence bar, split RC autogeneration, invalidation-impact preview). Targets Solve Stage Opening section in design-large-task SKILL.md plus proof MCP source where initialization signature changes. Inherits 22 Rules + 3 NCs + 11 Evidence + PERM-1 + 5 Risks from cluster-B umbrella. Reads cluster-B.2 outputs (closing-argument materialization mechanism, two-yes closure model, mutation-clears-flags discipline) as Rules.

problem_articulation.text: B.1's contract design has to structurally validate inbound material (per R4 — the caller does not validate) while not stripping the rationale, provenance, or conversational context the agent carried at transition time. Today's MCP tools were designed for an agent-internal turn-by-turn use pattern, not a generic single-transition intake. The single-transition reframe (R6) creates two design pressures pulling against each other: tight typed shapes for validation rigor, loose accommodation for caller-supplied context.
problem_articulation.current_behavior: no single-transition intake exists; tools assume turn-by-turn agent-driven calls; no provision for caller-supplied context to ride alongside typed material
problem_articulation.desired_behavior: single contract operation that validates structurally, accepts categorically-typed material, and carries caller-supplied context cleanly without forcing nuance into the typed shape

success_criteria.text: Contract surface defined comprehensively enough that the SKILL.md Solve Stage Opening section, the brief template Concerns section, and the proof MCP source changes are mechanical derivations from the contract spec rather than separate design decisions
success_criteria.observable: design brief enumerates contract operations with typed shapes, state transitions, opening-protocol rules, and inheritance behavior; downstream artifacts (SKILL.md text, brief template, code) cite the contract spec section as their source

success_criteria.text: Contract validates submissions structurally without rejecting nuance — any material a caller can express in the six categories is accepted; any context a caller considers load-bearing has a clean place to ride along (provenance, optional fields, or explicitly out-of-scope-at-boundary)
success_criteria.observable: callers do not lose rationale, provenance, or conversational context they consider load-bearing in order to satisfy the contract

success_criteria.text: Contract surface stays small enough that the boundary is comprehensible at a glance and not bureaucratic
success_criteria.anti_goal: the contract grows into a B-Method-style rigid clause structure that requires extensive caller ceremony

done_state_vision.text: Design brief states loose-middle as the locked contract rigor position, names the six categories with per-category required vs optional fields, and names the free-form metadata channel for caller-supplied context that downstream proof construction needs.
done_state_vision.imagined_moment: I open the cluster-b-1 design brief, see a table with six rows (one per category) listing required-fields and optional-fields, and a paragraph explaining the metadata channel's role in supplying construction context for Evidence in particular. I pass it to design-specify and the spec generation runs without me supplying additional context.

constraint_envelope.text: Phase 4b has no knowledge of Phase 4a
Phase 4b has a contract that any generic design system can submit information into; the contract is the only legal cross-boundary surface
Concerns are drafted in Phase 4a (4a-side production)
Phase 4a does not validate Concerns; structural validation is contract-side, ratification is designer-side
Sequential ratification preserved — each Resolve Condition ratified one at a time
Six categories the contract accepts are immutable: problem statement, Concerns, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Resolve Conditions; Necessary Conditions and Risks excluded
Phase 4b solve presumed valid and sufficient — default work is confirmation not refactor
Plugin architecture preserved — multiple Phase 4a implementations coexist; contract is generic over them
4a is out of scope for this sprint — no design discussion, vocabulary, or mechanism work crosses into 4a territory
4a makes a single submission to 4b — the submission IS the transition action between the two systems

scope_boundary.text: Updates to the Solve Stage Opening section in design-large-task SKILL.md (caller-facing documentation derived from the contract definition)
Brief template Concerns and Resolve Condition sections updated to reflect the Concern lifecycle (draft vs ratified)
scope_boundary.source: derives from Concern lifecycle but lives in template artifact

scope_boundary.text: CN/CERN ID-prefix inconsistency fix (RISK-4 carried forward)
scope_boundary.source: RISK-4 inherited from cluster-B handover; template fix not contract fix
cluster B.2 owns; B.1 emits state, B.2 reads it

scope_boundary.text: 4a-internal flow logic, mechanisms, and ratification-side responsibilities

4b-internal operations — Concern lock semantics, sequential RC ratification, four iteration mechanisms (merge bar, split autogen, late-lock gate, invalidation preview), state visibility for closing-side reads
Caller-context-preservation mechanism on the contract — how rationale, provenance, conversational thread ride alongside typed submission material

personal_use_case_map.text: Current session: defining Phase 4b's contract so the proof MCP can accept submissions cleanly without per-caller bespoke handling.
personal_use_case_map.scenario: Mid-session on Chester master plan rebuild-design-derivation, working through cluster B sub-sprints; need contract design settled before plan-build can be specced.

personal_use_case_map.text: Months from now, extending Phase 4b for a new caller type (e.g., a CLI tool, a different Understanding flow): the brief tells me the validation philosophy and metadata channel rules so I don't have to re-derive them from scratch.
personal_use_case_map.scenario: Returning to Chester after a gap to add a new caller; need to know whether the contract accepts the new shape without modification.
personal_use_case_map.context_assumed_lost: Specific reasoning chain that produced loose-middle vs tight; the six tensions debated this session; why metadata channel was chosen over typed context fields.

cost_energy_budget.text: Designer concern about overengineering: contract surface should err on the side of small + clear boundaries, not exhaustive enumeration of every conceivable case

Designer concern about preserving agent context: design effort spent on context-preservation mechanism is well-spent if it keeps the typed shape minimal; effort spent on widening the typed shape to absorb every kind of context is poorly spent

project_fit.text: Bureaucratic typed declaration patterns (B-Method-style rigid clauses, NASA RTM-style ceremony per requirement) misfit Chester's design-conversation aesthetic, which tolerates fluid material and trusts the agent + designer to handle nuance
project_fit.project_convention_referenced: Chester design-conversation aesthetic — plain language, peer talk, low ceremony
project_fit.override_reason: the contract still needs structural validation for safety per R4; the design must find the minimum-viable structural shape that preserves caller flexibility, not adopt heavyweight enterprise contract patterns

open_questions_ledger.text: Is defining the Phase 4b contract surface comprehensively the full B.1 scope, or are there design decisions outside the contract definition that B.1 also owns?

Is the late-lock procrastination gate contract-enforced (the contract refuses certain operations after N rounds without lock) or caller-enforced (each design system handles deferral discipline internally)?
How does the contract balance structural validation rigor (mandated by R4 — caller doesn't validate) against caller flexibility — what level of validation strictness is right?
How does the single transition submission preserve caller-supplied context (rationale, provenance, conversational thread, alternatives considered) without overstuffing the typed categories?
Does provenance metadata on each submitted item carry caller context, or do typed categories get optional context fields, or is caller context held in caller memory only and not part of the proof?
What's the minimum-viable structural shape — required category tag plus minimal required fields per category, or richer typed schemas, or somewhere between?

glossary.definition: The Understand stage of design-large-task. Produces raw, problem-side material in agent session memory.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked verbatim
Not 'Phase 4', not 'Understanding phase'

glossary.definition: The Solve stage of design-large-task. Consumes Phase 4a material into a typed proof structure under the proof MCP.

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that

Prior definition referenced "Phase 4a implementation makes contract submissions" and "specific Phase 4a implementations may submit in their own order" — leaks. Also "sequence" framing presupposed a single fixed order; the contract expresses opening constraints, not a sequence. Renaming "Initialization sequence" → "Opening protocol" with old name as alias.

glossary.definition: NC-1 from cluster-B umbrella. Six explicit Phase 4a → Phase 4b mappings, each with named responsibility: problem statement, Concerns, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Resolve Conditions. Necessary Conditions and Risks are excluded from the boundary.

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that

Also "pairings" implied 4a↔4b mapping shape now prohibited. Renaming "Six pairings" → "Six categories" with old name as alias for backward reference.

glossary.definition: NC-2 from cluster-B umbrella. Channel one is the problem statement carried explicitly into Phase 4b's entry. Channel two is everything else (draft Concerns, codebase observations, designer directives, identified Risks) accessible through agent session memory.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC; B.1 must revalidate but not re-derive

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into

The "explicit-parameter vs session-memory" distinction was meaningful only when Phase 4b could reach in; with all input flowing through the contract, the channel split dissolves. Replaced by Phase 4b contract + Contract submission.

glossary.definition: NC-3 from cluster-B umbrella. The Concern set is revised across Phase 4b as Evidence and Risk understanding shifts. Five integration constraints govern operations on Concerns and the Resolve Conditions anchored to them.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC; B.1 must revalidate but not re-derive

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval. Definition cleaned of Phase-4a leak ("as Evidence and Risk understanding shifts" → "as understanding shifts" — drops upstream-source assumption now that contract reframe makes producer agnostic). Flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Cluster-A discipline preserved into Concern iteration: each Resolve Condition is ratified one at a time. No batch approval, no gestalt sign-off.
sense_constraints: Inherited locked discipline

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster A. Inherited forward; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: First of NC-3's five integration constraints. A mechanism that prevents the designer from indefinitely deferring Concern lock as Phase 4b proceeds. Today, no such mechanism exists.
sense_constraints: Mechanism shape is open in B.1

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED with definition preserved.

glossary.definition: Second of NC-3's five integration constraints. Merging two Concerns into one requires Evidence citation supporting the collapse — merges may not happen on agent or designer instinct alone.
sense_constraints: Mechanism shape is open in B.1

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Third of NC-3's five integration constraints. When one Concern is split into two, the Resolve Conditions anchored to the original are autogenerated for each new Concern rather than left to manual re-anchoring.
sense_constraints: Mechanism shape is open in B.1

history.designer_quote: approved

history.designer_quote: approved

glossary.definition: B.2's locked deliverable. The materialized walk of the live Resolve Condition set produced at proof closure, gated by a composite trigger and the two-yes model. B.1 inherits this as the exit shape its initialization sequence must thread into.

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

history.designer_quote: and most of these probably dont apply to b1 planning, internal to b2
history.reason: B.2-internal. Closing argument is B.2's downstream deliverable; B.1's contract design doesn't engage with how it materializes. Will resurface at implementation time when B.1's contract integrates with B.2's closure mechanism, but not in B.1's design conversation vocabulary.

glossary.definition: B.2's closure pattern. Closure is permitted only when the designer presented and confirmed go for the closing argument in the same round; any subsequent state mutation clears both flags.
sense_constraints: Inherited as Rule from B.2

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

history.designer_quote: and most of these probably dont apply to b1 planning, internal to b2
history.reason: B.2-internal closure-gate machinery. Not a B.1 design-conversation concern. B.1 designs the contract; B.2 owns the closure pattern around mutations.

glossary.definition: B.2's invariant. Every state-mutating function calls clearClosingFlags at entry. Any new mutating operation B.1 introduces (such as Concern merge or split) inherits this requirement structurally.
sense_constraints: Inherited structural invariant from B.2

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

history.designer_quote: and most of these probably dont apply to b1 planning, internal to b2
history.reason: B.2-internal implementation invariant. From B.1's design altitude the contract just supports operations; how B.2 wires flag-clearing around mutations is downstream implementation detail. Surfaces at spec/plan time, not in design vocabulary.

glossary.definition: Master plan working name for the Phase 4a output shape that flows into Phase 4b. Cluster B is settling the actual transition mechanism; this term may be superseded by what B.1 ratifies.
sense_constraints: Working name only; revisable in B.1

history.designer_quote: phase 4b can not depend on any phase 4a structured data / phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Designer Rule: Phase 4b cannot depend on Phase 4a structured data. The term presumed Phase 4b reaches in to consume a Phase 4a output shape — the new contract reframe makes that direction prohibited. Term dissolves entirely.

glossary.definition: Phase 4a-side ledger that preserves rejected solve-mode statements as input to Phase 4b. Today the ledger is declared in the problemfocused flow but has no consuming step in the Solve Stage Opening.
sense_constraints: Currently declared-but-unconsumed (RISK-5 candidate)

history.designer_quote: phase 4b can not depend on any phase 4a structured data
history.reason: Designer Rule prohibits Phase 4b reaching into Phase 4a structures. Ledger remains a problemfocused-internal construct; any cross-boundary use must flow through the contract.

history.designer_quote: what is this and why is still around? Solve Leakage Ledger
history.reason: Designer challenge: term has no 4b-side role under R1 (4b has no knowledge of 4a). Originally seeded only because cluster-B handover EVID-7 flagged it as "declared-but-unconsumed" → RISK-5 candidate. R1 + R2 dissolve that risk: any ledger emission becomes a 4a-implementation-internal choice (translate ledger entries into Evidence submissions or hold locally), opaque to Phase 4b. Term is not B.1's design subject and not in the contract surface. Drop.

glossary.definition: The six raw-material categories the Phase 4b contract accepts via submission, regardless of which Phase 4a implementation produced them: problem statement, Concerns, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Resolve Conditions. Necessary Conditions and Risks are excluded from the contract surface.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC from cluster B; identity of the six items locked
Definition reframed by designer Rule: contract-mediated, not Phase 4a→Phase 4b mapping

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Designer Rule reframes the relationship: the six items are no longer pairings between two structured sides; they are categories the generic contract accepts. Identity of the six locked; relational shape revised.

glossary.definition: The Phase 4b contract surface plus the order in which a Phase 4a implementation makes contract submissions to open Phase 4b. B.1 is designing the contract surface and the canonical sequence; specific Phase 4a implementations may submit in their own order subject to contract rules.
sense_constraints: Today's Solve Stage Opening is three steps and addresses only the problem-statement category
Sequence is per-implementation; contract surface is universal

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Designer Rule reframes: not a fixed handoff sequence but a contract surface plus per-implementation ordering subject to contract rules.

glossary.definition: Problemfocused-implementation-specific construct that captures rejected solve-mode statements during Phase 4a. The implementation may choose to submit ledger entries through the Phase 4b contract (as Evidence or otherwise) or hold them locally; Phase 4b does not reach into the ledger.
sense_constraints: Phase 4b makes no assumption about its existence — only the problemfocused implementation maintains it
If consumed, must arrive via contract submission like any other material

history.designer_quote: phase 4b can not depend on any phase 4a structured data
history.reason: Designer Rule prohibits Phase 4b reaching into Phase 4a structures. Ledger remains a problemfocused-internal construct; any cross-boundary use must flow through the contract.

glossary.definition: The submission interface Phase 4b exposes for any Phase 4a implementation to deposit raw material. Channel-agnostic, structure-agnostic — the contract knows nothing about the producing implementation's internal shape. Categorical: each submission carries one of the six raw-material categories plus the producer's claim of category.
sense_constraints: Designer Rule: Phase 4b cannot depend on any Phase 4a structured data — the contract is the only legal cross-boundary surface
B.1's primary design subject

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Designer-issued Rule introduces the contract as the sole legal cross-boundary surface. Term needed to refer to it canonically.

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction: previous definition still mentioned "Phase 4a implementation" and "raw material" — both leak the prohibited structural dependency. Rewritten to describe contract purely by what it exposes and how it validates.

history.designer_quote: rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a
history.reason: Designer Rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a. Prior sense_constraint mentioned "Phase 4a" twice — even saying "no reference to Phase 4a" is a reference. Cleaned to self-describing language with no caller-domain terms.

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 reduces the contract to a single intake action. Prior definition implied a multi-operation API surface. Revised to single-transition framing; explicitly notes post-transition operations are 4b-internal.

glossary.definition: Any pluggable Understand-stage flow (classic, problemfocused, team-interview, future ones) that produces raw material and calls into the Phase 4b contract. The contract is generic over implementations — none gets privileged access.
sense_constraints: Plugin-architecture term: aligns with master plan §4.3 preserving multi-MCP / multi-flow arrangement
Generic — no specific implementation may be assumed by Phase 4b

history.designer_quote: any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Designer-issued Rule references "any generic 4a implementation" — needs canonical term for the category of pluggable producers.

history.designer_quote: draft concerns are a 4a consideration; adhere to the rules and dont cross boundaries. why are we trying to implement 4a considerations. Rule: 4a is out of scope for this sprint
history.reason: 4a-side concept, out of scope for B.1 per R5. Was kept as a contextual term but R5 + boundary-discipline correction makes it active vocabulary leak. Contract caller (already DEFINED) is the 4b-side noun for "anything that submits"; Design system is the broader caller category. Neither requires referencing Phase 4a implementations.

glossary.definition: A single act of a Phase 4a implementation calling the Phase 4b contract with one raw-material item. The submission carries the item, its claimed category (one of the six), and provenance. The contract validates and accepts or rejects; it does not interpret.
sense_constraints: Validation is structural (category shape, required fields), not semantic (Phase 4b doesn't judge the content's quality)
Atomic — one submission carries one item

history.designer_quote: phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into
history.reason: Need a canonical noun for the act of submitting through the contract — distinguishes from the contract surface itself and from the raw material.

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction: prior definition named "Phase 4a implementation calling" — leak. Rewritten as inbound call from any caller.

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 introduces single-submission semantics. Prior definition framed submission as "one item per call" which presumed a stream of submissions over time. Renaming to "Transition submission" and defining it as the single comprehensive transition action.

glossary.definition: The submission interface Phase 4b exposes. Accepts items in one of the six categories with category-specific required fields and provenance; returns acceptance or structured rejection plus updated proof state. Validation is structural; content is not interpreted. Stateful — submissions accumulate proof state across the session. The contract is the only legal ingress to Phase 4b state.
sense_constraints: Definition makes no reference to Phase 4a or any Phase 4a structure — the contract is self-describing
B.1's primary design subject

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction: previous definition still mentioned "Phase 4a implementation" and "raw material" — both leak the prohibited structural dependency. Rewritten to describe contract purely by what it exposes and how it validates.

glossary.definition: A single atomic inbound call to the Phase 4b contract carrying one item, its claimed category (one of the six), and provenance metadata. Returns acceptance or structured rejection. Validated structurally; not interpreted.
sense_constraints: Definition makes no reference to Phase 4a as the caller — callers are anonymous to the contract
Atomic — one submission carries one item

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction: prior definition named "Phase 4a implementation calling" — leak. Rewritten as inbound call from any caller.

glossary.definition: The six categories the Phase 4b contract accepts: problem statement, Concerns, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Resolve Conditions. Necessary Conditions and Risks are excluded from the contract surface.
sense_constraints: Identity of the six items locked from cluster B NC-1

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction + rename. Prior definition mentioned "raw-material categories" and "regardless of which Phase 4a implementation produced them" — leaks. Also "pairings" implied 4a↔4b mapping shape now prohibited. Renaming "Six pairings" → "Six categories" with old name as alias for backward reference.

glossary.definition: The portion of the Phase 4b contract that governs session opening — which categorical submissions the contract requires, allows, or refuses before per-turn operation begins. Defines preconditions and ordering constraints in terms the contract enforces, not in terms of any caller's internal flow.
sense_constraints: Today's Solve Stage Opening is three caller-side steps coupling to Evidence/Rules categories — the new protocol expresses opening rules from the contract side only
Renamed from 'Initialization sequence' — the prior name implied a single sequence; the protocol expresses constraints, not a fixed sequence

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Designer correction + rename. Also "sequence" framing presupposed a single fixed order; the contract expresses opening constraints, not a sequence. Renaming "Initialization sequence" → "Opening protocol" with old name as alias.

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 collapses what was a multi-operation ordering question into a single-submission validation question. Opening protocol now describes the rules governing the one transition submission, not ordering across multiple operations.

glossary.definition: From the Phase 4b contract's standpoint, anything that issues a contract submission. Anonymous — the contract distinguishes callers only by submission provenance metadata, never by their internal type or category. The Phase 4a side may organize callers into implementations (classic, problemfocused, team-interview) but Phase 4b sees only callers.
sense_constraints: The 4b-side noun for the role 'Phase 4a implementation' plays from the 4a side
Use this term in any 4b-side definition to avoid leaking 'Phase 4a implementation'

history.designer_quote: why do we still have any references to any 4a structure in the definitions? rule should prevent that
history.reason: Need a 4b-side noun for callers so future definitions don't drift back to "Phase 4a implementation" framing. Phase 4a implementation remains valid as a 4a-side term; Contract caller is its 4b-side counterpart.

history.designer_quote: rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a
history.reason: Designer Rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a. Prior definition mentioned "Phase 4a side" and named specific implementations (classic, problemfocused, team-interview) — leaks. Cleaned: caller is anonymous, 4b knows no further taxonomy.

glossary.definition: The Solve stage of design-large-task. Builds a typed proof structure from items received through its contract.

history.reason: Designer Rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a. Prior sense_constraint mentioned "Phase 4a" twice — even saying "no reference to Phase 4a" is a reference. Cleaned to self-describing language with no caller-domain terms.

glossary.definition: From the Phase 4b contract's standpoint, anything that issues a contract submission. Anonymous — the contract distinguishes callers only by submission provenance metadata, never by their internal type or category.
sense_constraints: The 4b-side noun for any submitter; 4b knows no further taxonomy beyond this single category
Use this term in any 4b-side definition to avoid leaking caller-domain terms

history.designer_quote: rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a
history.reason: Designer Rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a. Prior definition mentioned "Phase 4a side" and named specific implementations (classic, problemfocused, team-interview) — leaks. Cleaned: caller is anonymous, 4b knows no further taxonomy.

glossary.definition: The designer's confirmed hypothesis about what is broken or being solved. One of the six categories the Phase 4b contract accepts.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked verbatim
Not a solution shape; describes the pain

history.designer_quote: rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a
history.reason: Designer Rule: 4b has no knowledge of 4a. Prior definition said "Carried into Phase 4b as the explicit channel-one input" — vestige of deprecated two-channel framing and implies a flow direction. Cleaned to category-only description.

glossary.definition: Any system that produces design material and submits it through the Phase 4b contract. Phase 4a is one such design system; others (CLI tools, web forms, automated fixtures, alternative design pipelines) may also call the contract. The contract is generic over design systems — none gets privileged access.
sense_constraints: Caller category named in R2
Broader than 'Phase 4a implementation' — Phase 4b is reusable across multiple design systems, not coupled to design-large-task's particular Understand→Solve pipeline

history.designer_quote: r2 - phase 4b has a contract that any generic design systme can submit information into
history.reason: Designer-revised R2 names "generic design system" as the caller category. Term needed to refer canonically. Generalizes beyond Phase 4a implementation — Phase 4b becomes reusable across any design system that produces submitted material.

glossary.definition: The Understand stage of design-large-task. Produces raw, problem-side material in agent session memory.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked verbatim by master plan
Inherited; not subject to revision in B.1

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by master plan vocabulary. Not pending B.1 ratification. Definition preserved verbatim; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: A PM-ratified issue anchored to the problem statement. Concerns are enumerated and locked at Phase 4b opening; each Concern anchors at least one Resolve Condition (or is covered by a Rule) for the closure gate.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked from cluster A; inherited
ID prefix CERN-N in code, CN-N in brief template (RISK-4 inconsistency carried forward)

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster A. Inherited forward; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Sixth proof element type. PM-readable observable claim that, when ratified, certifies a Concern is addressed. Three fields: statement, problem_anchor (Concern ID), ratification.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked from cluster A; inherited
Five locked attributes: observable, designer-ratified, problem-statement-anchored, forward-looking, non-restrictive

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster A. Inherited forward; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Cluster-A discipline preserved into Concern iteration: each Resolve Condition is ratified one at a time. No batch approval, no gestalt sign-off.
sense_constraints: Designer-locked from cluster A; inherited
Carried into B.1 unchanged

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster A. Inherited forward; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: B.2's locked deliverable. The materialized walk of the live Resolve Condition set produced at proof closure, gated by a composite trigger and the two-yes model. B.1 inherits as the exit shape its design must thread into.
sense_constraints: Locked by cluster B.2; inherited as forward Rule
Not subject to revision in B.1

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: B.2's closure pattern. Closure permitted only when designer presented and confirmed go for the closing argument in the same round; any subsequent state mutation clears both flags.
sense_constraints: Locked by cluster B.2 (NCON-4); inherited as forward Rule
Not subject to revision in B.1

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: B.2's invariant. Every state-mutating function clears the two-yes flags at entry. Any new mutating operation B.1 introduces inherits this requirement structurally.
sense_constraints: Locked by cluster B.2 (NCON-4 / AC-5.5); inherited as forward Rule
Structural invariant — applies to any mutation B.1 designs

history.designer_quote: also, lots of sprint b2 terms embedded that should be locked from previous
history.reason: Status correction: locked by cluster B.2. Inherited forward as Rule; B.1 does not re-ratify. Definition preserved; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: The Concern set is revised across Phase 4b's session as understanding shifts. Five integration constraints govern operations on Concerns and the Resolve Conditions anchored to them. Umbrella term for the four mechanism candidates B.1 designs.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC-3 from cluster B; ratified en-bloc by designer in B.1 Round One
Mechanism shapes for the four constraints below remain B.1's design subject

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval. Definition cleaned of Phase-4a leak ("as Evidence and Risk understanding shifts" → "as understanding shifts" — drops upstream-source assumption now that contract reframe makes producer agnostic). Flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: First of NC-3's five integration constraints. A contract-side mechanism preventing the designer from indefinitely deferring Concern lock as Phase 4b proceeds. Today no such mechanism exists.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC-3a; ratified by designer en-bloc in B.1 Round One
Mechanism shape is B.1's design subject

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED with definition preserved.

glossary.definition: Second of NC-3's five integration constraints. Merging two Concerns into one requires Evidence citation supporting the collapse — merges may not happen on agent or designer instinct alone.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC-3b; ratified by designer en-bloc in B.1 Round One
Mechanism shape is B.1's design subject

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Third of NC-3's five integration constraints. When one Concern is split into two, the Resolve Conditions anchored to the original are autogenerated for each new Concern rather than left to manual re-anchoring.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC-3c; ratified by designer en-bloc in B.1 Round One
Mechanism shape is B.1's design subject

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: Fourth of NC-3's five integration constraints (the fifth is sequential ratification preserved). Before a merge or split commits, the designer sees which existing Resolve Conditions would lose ratification as a result.
sense_constraints: Inherited NC-3d; ratified by designer en-bloc in B.1 Round One
Mechanism shape is B.1's design subject

history.designer_quote: approved
history.reason: Designer en-bloc approval; flipping to DEFINED.

glossary.definition: A Concern in the pre-ratification state — submitted to the Phase 4b contract by a caller but not yet designer-ratified. Validates structurally on submission per contract rules; does not yet anchor Resolve Conditions or contribute to closure coverage. Becomes a ratified Concern via designer action.
sense_constraints: Names the lifecycle state R3 + R4 introduce: 4a-drafted, contract-accepted, designer-not-yet-ratified
Distinct from ratified Concern (cluster-A canonical) which anchors RCs and contributes to closure

history.designer_quote: rule: concerns are drafted in 4a / rule: 4a does not validate concerns
history.reason: R3 + R4 introduce a Concern lifecycle: 4a drafts, 4a does not validate, contract validates structurally, designer ratifies. The pre-ratification state needs a name so we can talk about contract operations on drafts (submission, structural validation, designer ratification transition) distinct from operations on ratified Concerns (anchor RCs, lock, merge with evidence).

history.designer_quote: draft concerns are a 4a consideration; adhere to the rules and dont cross boundaries. why are we trying to implement 4a considerations. Rule: 4a is out of scope for this sprint
history.reason: Boundary violation. Per R3+R4, drafting happens in 4a; the draft state lives on the 4a side, not the contract side. R5 (4a out of scope for this sprint) makes the 4a-side state explicitly off-limits for B.1. The contract receives Concerns through submission and treats them per cluster A's model (added → lockable → anchors RCs); whatever pre-submission state existed is opaque to the contract. Term dissolves entirely.

glossary.definition: The single comprehensive inbound submission to the Phase 4b contract that effects the transition from any caller into Phase 4b. Carries all material the caller intends to deposit — problem statement plus any of the six categories the caller has produced — in one atomic call. Returns acceptance or structured rejection plus the initial Phase 4b state.
sense_constraints: Per R6: 4a makes a single submission to 4b; the submission IS the transition action between the two systems
After the transition, no further caller submissions occur — all subsequent activity is 4b-internal

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 introduces single-submission semantics. Prior definition framed submission as "one item per call" which presumed a stream of submissions over time. Renaming to "Transition submission" and defining it as the single comprehensive transition action.

glossary.definition: The single transition-intake interface Phase 4b exposes. Accepts one comprehensive transition submission containing material in any of the six categories with category-specific required fields and provenance; returns acceptance or structured rejection plus the initial Phase 4b state. Validation is structural; content is not interpreted. The contract is the only legal cross-boundary surface.
sense_constraints: Per R6: a single transition action, not a multi-operation surface
Self-describing — uses no terms from any caller's domain
Post-transition operations on Phase 4b state are 4b-internal, not contract operations

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 reduces the contract to a single intake action. Prior definition implied a multi-operation API surface. Revised to single-transition framing; explicitly notes post-transition operations are 4b-internal.

glossary.definition: The rules the Phase 4b contract enforces on the transition submission — which categories are required, which are optional, what required fields each category must carry, what structural validation runs, what initial Phase 4b state is produced on acceptance.
sense_constraints: Per R6: applies to the single transition submission, not a sequence of operations
Defines preconditions and validation rules, not multi-step ordering

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 collapses what was a multi-operation ordering question into a single-submission validation question. Opening protocol now describes the rules governing the one transition submission, not ordering across multiple operations.

glossary.definition: Any operation on Phase 4b state that occurs after the transition submission — designer ratification of Resolve Conditions, Concern lock, Concern iteration mechanisms (merge, split, invalidation preview, late-lock gate), closing-argument-related state mutations. Not a contract operation; not driven by caller submission.
sense_constraints: Per R6: distinct from the single contract transition; lives entirely inside Phase 4b
Designer interactions with Phase 4b post-transition fall in this category, as do agent operations on the proof state

history.designer_quote: rule; 4a makes a single submission to 4b (transition action between the two systems)
history.reason: R6 creates a sharp distinction between contract (single transition surface) and 4b-internal mechanisms (everything post-transition). Need a canonical term for the latter category so we can discuss 4b-internal mechanism design without confusing it with contract operations.

vocabularyActionLog.reason: Designer Rule: Phase 4b cannot depend on Phase 4a structured data. The term presumed Phase 4b reaches in to consume a Phase 4a output shape — the new contract reframe makes that direction prohibited. Term dissolves entirely.
vocabularyActionLog.designer_quote: phase 4b can not depend on any phase 4a structured data / phase 4b has a contract that any generic 4a implementation can submit information into

vocabularyActionLog.reason: Designer Rule: contract reframe collapses both channels into one. The "explicit-parameter vs session-memory" distinction was meaningful only when Phase 4b could reach in; with all input flowing through the contract, the channel split dissolves. Replaced by Phase 4b contract + Contract submission.

entry.text: Today's Solve Stage Opening describes three caller-side steps that address only the problem statement plus initial Evidence and Rule seeding. The other four of the six categories the Phase 4b contract accepts (Concerns, Resolve Conditions, Permissions, and the draft-vs-ratified lifecycle on Concerns) have no documented protocol. The gap shows up as caller ambiguity at session opening and at every subsequent moment a Concern, RC, or Permission comes into being.
entry.current_behavior: skill text covers a subset of the six categories; code accepts more than the text instructs; callers fall back on agent inference for the rest
entry.desired_behavior: the Phase 4b contract is defined comprehensively enough that the skill text becomes a mechanical derivation of contract operations rather than the authoritative source

repeatBackHistory.statement: Phase 4b exposes a contract surface where a single submission opens the proof. The contract receives six categories of material — Necessary Conditions, Evidence, Rules, Permissions, Risks, Concerns. Contract structure must balance two tensions: how rigorously to validate incoming submissions (working position: loose-middle, validate only fields load-bearing for downstream operations), and how much caller-supplied context is required to build the proof internal data structures (evidence in particular).
